text: Welcome to this educational video on laparoscopic restorative proctocolectomy and ileal pouch anal anastomosis done for ulcerative colitis. In the operating room, the patient is placed in a lithotomy position with both the arms tucked by the side of the patient. Care is taken to prevent hypothermia and DBT prevention is done by using stockings and heparin. The monitor is on the left side of the patient. The surgeon stands on the right side of the patient and the camera operator is to the left side of the surgeon. The first assistant is to the left side of the patient. It is useful to have monitors on both the sides of the patient for this surgery. Special instruments needed for total restorative proctocolectomy include a traumatic bowel endoclinch forceps and various types of staplers including roticulator endocutting staplers usually two cartridges linear cutting staplers two cartridges to create a pouch and one circular stapler either a twenty eight or twenty nine number to create the pouch anal anastomosis about five to six stroke hours are needed for this operation the camera is placed at the umbilicus with a zero degree telescope. The two surgeon's working ports are placed on the right side of the abdomen, the ten millimeter port placed in the right iliac fossa at the site of ileostomy, and a five millimeter port placed in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen just above the umbilicus. Both these ports are in the midclavicular line with the right port slightly lateral to the left port. The two assistant grasping ports are placed in the left iliac fossa and the left upper abdomen and both these ports are five millimeter ports. A five millimeter suprapubic port is optional and is used for retraction during the rectal dissection.

The surgeon carefully places the small bowel loops out of the pelvis, then lifts and stretches the sigmoid mesocolon and mesorectum. The right ureter is identified, and the pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery and superior hemorrhoidal artery is stretched.

The surgeon makes an incision on the peritoneum from the sacral promontory in the midline, extending downwards onto the mesorectum and upwards parallel to the aorta. The vascular pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery is identified and dissected free from the surrounding areolar tissue. The sympathetic nerve trunk is identified and preserved. The inferior mesenteric artery is further dissected and skeletonized for the application of metallic or locking clips, which are then used to clip and divide the artery.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the posterior avascular plane between the sigmoid mesocolon and retroperitoneal structures, identifying and dissecting the ureter and gonadal vessels. The dissection extends laterally to the lateral peritoneal attachment, superiorly to mobilize the splenic flexure, and inferiorly in the pre-sacral avascular plane for rectal dissection, ensuring the left ureter is safeguarded.

The surgeon divides the right lateral peritoneal folds with minimal bleeding, removes the mesorectum along with the rectum, and safeguards the hypogastric sympathetic nerves. The peritoneal fold anterior to the rectum is divided in the rectovaginal pouch in females and the rectovesical pouch in males. Dissection continues posteriorly in the presacral space to the pelvic floor, with the right lateral ligaments divided to separate the rectum from the pelvic cavity. Anteriorly, the rectum is separated from the vagina in females and from the prostate and seminal vesicle in males, continuing the dissection down to the pelvic floor. Adequate retraction and traction of the rectum are maintained throughout the procedure.

The surgeon divides the left peritoneal attachments and left lateral ligaments to reach the pelvic floor. The anterior dissection is completed, and the lateral peritoneal attachments of the sigmoid and descending colon are divided to mobilize the splenic flexure from its peritoneal and retroperitoneal attachments.

The surgeon mobilizes the ileocecal junction from its lateral peritoneal attachment, extending the dissection to the hepatic flexure of the colon. The hepatic flexure is fully mobilized. The rectum is divided at the pelvic floor using 45mm endocutting staplers, requiring two cartridges for complete division. The rectum is divided flush to the pelvic floor, leaving a small cuff for a double stapled anastomosis. A small midline incision, approximately 5-6 cm, is made in the peri-umbilical region to deliver the mobilized rectum through a mini laparotomy.

The surgeon divides the transverse mesocolon from the transverse colon and ligates the vascular pedicles supplying the colon. The ileocecal junction is divided, and the terminal ileum is closed in two layers. Stay sutures are placed on the folded terminal ileum to create a 15 cm ileal J pouch. The ileum is opened, and two fires of linear cutting staplers are used to form the J pouch. The circular stapler anvil is inserted, and a purse string is placed to secure the anvil. The J pouch is returned to the abdomen, and the neoperitoneum is recreated after closing the abdominal wall.

Under telescopic vision, the surgeon passes a circular stapler through the rectal cuff and approximates the anvil into the stapler. The ileal J pouch is aligned with the rectal cuff, ensuring the pouch and its mesentery are correctly positioned without tension on the stapler line. The stapler is fired to create the J pouch anal anastomosis, which is then checked for leakage. A proximal diverting ileostomy is performed, and the mini laparotomy wound is closed to complete the procedure.
